coredata.dc:title: Combustion synthesis of TiN–TiB2 composites in Ti/BN/N2 and Ti/BN/B reaction systems
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yeh, C.L.
Teng, G.S.
coredata.dc:description: An experimental study on in situ formation of TiN–TiB2 composites with a wide range of compositions by self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) was conducted in two reaction systems. Combustion of sample compacts made up of titanium (Ti) and boron nitride (BN) powders was initiated under nitrogen pressure for the synthesis of composites with TiN contents of 75–87.5mol%. Experiments with reactant compacts from the Ti–BN–B powder blends were performed in argon (Ar) for the preparation of composites with 50–75mol% of TiB2. Self-sustained reactions featuring a distinct combustion front were observed in both systems. Due to the involvement of solid and gaseous reagents in the former reaction system, sample porosity and dilution by TiN made a significant impact on the degree of nitridation. On the contrary, combustion synthesis in the latter test configuration was essentially governed by the reaction between solid constituents. Experimental results show direct formation of a composite with 75mol% TiN in nitrogen of 1.48MPa from an undiluted compact of 2Ti+BN at 60% TMD. For the preparation of composites with TiN contents as high as 83 and 87.5mol%, complete conversion was achieved by 60% TMD TiN-diluted samples with starting compositions of 2.5Ti+BN+0.5TiN and 3Ti+BN+TiN, respectively, at 1.14MPa of nitrogen. Through the use of elemental boron as one of the reactants along with the implementation of SHS reactions in Ar, TiN–TiB2 composites with TiB2 mole fractions of 50–75% were obtained from the Ti–BN–B powder compacts at different stoichiometries. In addition, complete conversion was also observed on a 1.5Ti+BN compact which yielded the composite with a molar ratio TiN/TiB2 =67/33 under an Ar environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiN–TiB2 composite
SHS
BN
Dilution
Flame-front velocity

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recorded burning images illustrating SHS processes associated with: (a) a 60% TMD sample of 3Ti+BN in 1.14MPa N2 and (b) a 60% TMD sample of 2Ti+BN+B in Ar.
Effects of nitrogen pressure, reactant composition, and sample density on flame-front propagation velocity of undiluted samples for synthesis of TiN–TiB2 composites in nitrogen.
Effects of nitrogen pressure and diluent content on flame-front propagation velocity of samples for synthesis of TiN–TiB2 composites in nitrogen.
Effect of starting composition on flame-front propagation velocity of Ti–BN–B reactant compacts for synthesis of TiN–TiB2 composites in argon.
Effects of nitrogen pressure, reactant composition, and sample density on nitridation percentage of undiluted samples for synthesis of TiN–TiB2 composites in nitrogen.
XRD patterns of combustion products synthesized from undiluted samples with different initial compositions of 2Ti+BN, 3Ti+BN, and 4Ti+BN in 1.14MPa N2.
Effects of nitrogen pressure, sample density, and diluent content on nitridation percentage of samples for synthesis of TiN–TiB2 composites in nitrogen.
XRD patterns of TiN–TiB2 composites with TiN contents from 75 to 87.5mol% synthesized from 60% TMD Ti–BN and Ti–BN–TiN reactant compacts in 1.14MPa N2.
XRD patterns of TiN–TiB2 composites with TiB2 contents from 33 to 75mol% synthesized from 60% TMD Ti–BN and Ti–BN–B reactant compacts in Ar.
Flame-front propagation velocity of sample compacts under different starting stoichiometries, green densities, and nitrogen pressures

article.ce:title: Combustion synthesis of TiN–TiB2 composites in Ti/BN/N2 and Ti/BN/B reaction systems

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Automation Engineering, Da-Yeh University, 112 Shan-Jiau Rd., Da-Tsuen, Changhua 51505, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An experimental study on in situ formation of TiN–TiB2 composites with a wide range of compositions by self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) was conducted in two reaction systems. Combustion of sample compacts made up of titanium (Ti) and boron nitride (BN) powders was initiated under nitrogen pressure for the synthesis of composites with TiN contents of 75–87.5mol%. Experiments with reactant compacts from the Ti–BN–B powder blends were performed in argon (Ar) for the preparation of composites with 50–75mol% of TiB2. Self-sustained reactions featuring a distinct combustion front were observed in both systems. Due to the involvement of solid and gaseous reagents in the former reaction system, sample porosity and dilution by TiN made a significant impact on the degree of nitridation. On the contrary, combustion synthesis in the latter test configuration was essentially governed by the reaction between solid constituents. Experimental results show direct formation of a composite with 75mol% TiN in nitrogen of 1.48MPa from an undiluted compact of 2Ti+BN at 60% TMD. For the preparation of composites with TiN contents as high as 83 and 87.5mol%, complete conversion was achieved by 60% TMD TiN-diluted samples with starting compositions of 2.5Ti+BN+0.5TiN and 3Ti+BN+TiN, respectively, at 1.14MPa of nitrogen. Through the use of elemental boron as one of the reactants along with the implementation of SHS reactions in Ar, TiN–TiB2 composites with TiB2 mole fractions of 50–75% were obtained from the Ti–BN–B powder compacts at different stoichiometries. In addition, complete conversion was also observed on a 1.5Ti+BN compact which yielded the composite with a molar ratio TiN/TiB2 =67/33 under an Ar environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Ceramic materials such as borides, nitrides, and carbides are potential candidates for advanced structural applications due to their exceptional hardness and stability at high temperatures. With the aim of achieving better and desired properties, the development of ceramic matrix composites that successfully combine the advantageous properties of individual components is of special interest to the ceramic industry [1–7]. For example, when TiB2 is combined with TiN, the composite exhibits several advantages such as high hardness and fracture toughness over the single phase ceramics [6,7]. In addition, the high electrical and thermal conductivities of TiB2, TiC, and TiN make TiB2–TiC and TiB2–TiN composites attractive for functional applications in high performing electrical systems [2].

Combustion synthesis with the merits of time and energy savings, simplicity, and high-purity products, has been recognized as a promising alternative to the conventional methods of producing advanced materials, including borides, carbides, nitrides, hydrides, silicides, carbonitrides, intermetallics, etc. [8–12]. Combustion synthesis can be conducted in either of two modes, the self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) and the thermal explosion. Moreover, combustion synthesis represents an in situ processing technique for the preparation of composite materials with interpenetrating phase microstructures and, hence, improved properties [1].

In addition to direct formation from elemental powders as a result of highly exothermic reactions, many boride-containing ceramic composites were shown to be produced by using boron carbide (B4C) or boron nitride (BN) as the reactant in a reactive synthesis process [1,2,13–16]. Gutmanas and Gotman [1] demonstrated the production of composites TiC–TiB2 and TiN–TiB2 by thermal explosion with compacted specimens from the 3Ti+B4C and 3Ti+2BN powder blends, respectively. Similarly, with the use of stoichiometric powder compacts consisting of 3Zr+B4C and 3Zr+2BN, ceramic composites ZrB2–ZrC and ZrB2–ZrN were produced by means of the reactive hot pressing in a graphite die at 1900°C and under 30MPa of Ar for 60min [13]. Shibuya et al. [14] also reported that dense TiN–TiB2 composites with relatively high hardness and toughness were fabricated by combustion synthesis from powder compacts made up of Ti and BN under high pressures of nitrogen. Their results showed the need of a high BN content (the molar ratio BN/Ti=1.0) to yield a stoichiometric nitride phase TiN in nitrogen of 4MPa [14].

The goal of this study was to achieve in situ formation of TiN–TiB2 composites with a wide range of compositions through well-designed experiments in two different reaction systems. For the synthesis of composite compounds with a high content of TiN, sample compacts prepared from the Ti–BN powder blends were used and the corresponding experiments were performed under high pressures of nitrogen. In contrast, composites with a high content of TiB2 were synthesized from the test specimens comprising Ti, BN, and B powders in an argon environment. In both reaction systems, the effect of starting stoichiometry of the reactant on the combustion characteristics as well as on the final composition of synthesized products was studied. Additionally, experimental parameters including nitrogen pressure, sample density, and dilution by TiN were investigated for the experiments conducted in nitrogen, due to the nature of reaction involving both solid and gaseous reagents.

The SHS reaction to produce TiN–TiB2 composite from powder compacts was conducted in a stainless-steel windowed combustion chamber. Details of the experimental setup and measurement approach were reported previously [17–20]. In this study, experiments were performed in two different test configurations; one was carried out under high pressures of nitrogen with the use of compacted reactants from the Ti–BN powder blends, and the other was conducted with Ti–BN–B powder compacts in argon. Manipulation of test conditions was based on a product composition TiN:TiB2 =0.67:0.33, which is representative of full conversion from a powder reactant composed of 1.5Ti+BN under an argon (Ar) environment. That is, the former type of the reaction systems was adopted for the synthesis of composites containing a TiN content higher than 67mol%. When the composites with a TiB2 content greater than 33mol% were required, the latter was employed. Detailed descriptions of these two reaction systems are given below.

Titanium (Strem Chemicals, −325mesh, 99% purity) and boron nitride (Strem Chemicals, 99% purity) powders were used as the solid reactants in this category of the investigation. Titanium and boron nitride powders were dry mixed in a ball mill for 10h with the molar ratio according to the stoichiometry of the following reaction:
(1)
xTi+BN+0.5(x −1.5)N2 →(x −0.5)TiN+0.5TiB2
Reaction (1) signifies the reaction taking place under gaseous nitrogen due to the need of nitrogen from both the reactant BN and the surroundings. The pressure of nitrogen (with a purity of 99.999%) varied from 0.45 to 1.48MPa in this study. The reactant BN not only contributes boron to the formation of TiB2, but provides a solid source of nitrogen for the synthesis of TiN. The x value in reaction (1) represents the molar ratio of Ti/BN in the green compact. In this study, homogenous powder blends with three different molar compositions (including x =2.0, 3.0, and 4.0) were prepared for the synthesis of TiN–TiB2 composites with the TiN content between 75 and 87.5mol%. Due to the nature of the reaction involving solid and gaseous reagents, mixed powders in a stainless-steel mold were uniaxially pressed at pressures of about 40–70MPa into cylindrical samples (having a diameter of 7mm and a height of 12mm) with density ranging from 45 to 60% of the theoretical maximum density (TMD) of powder mixture in order to investigate the effect of sample porosity on combustion. The amount of nitrogen uptake after combustion was calculated from the measurement of weight change of the sample compact. The degree of nitridation was evaluated on the basis of the assumptions that the reaction between Ti and B in the sample to form TiB2 is complete owing to the very high affinity of Ti for boron [1], and that nitrogen reacts with available titanium to form stoichiometric nitride TiN [20].

As reported by previous studies [19–21], excessive melting of Ti was typically observed in the SHS reaction with gaseous nitrogen, which impeded the infiltration of nitrogen gas because of the diminution of sample porosity and in turn lowered the degree of nitridation. Therefore, dilution of the reactant mixture with the nitride product was suggested as an effective means to reduce the combustion temperature and thus to improve the extent of nitridation. On the preparation of test samples containing titanium nitride TiN (Strem Chemicals, −325mesh, 99% purity) as the diluent, the composition of reactant compacts was based upon the following reaction:
(2) (x − y)Ti+BN+ yTiN+0.5(x − y −1.5)N2 →(x −0.5)TiN+0.5TiB2
The diluent content required in the powder mixture depended upon the composition of desired products. The coefficient y in reaction (2) representing the degree of dilution was varied between 0.2 and 1.0 in this study.

Reactant compacts adopted under this reaction system were composed of Ti, BN, and amorphous boron (Noah Technologies Corp., 92% purity) powders. The particle size of boron powders is specified as 1μm maximum per Fisher sub-sieve sizer (FSSS). Similar to typical amorphous boron, the major impurities in the boron specimen used in this study include magnesium (Mg) about 5.0%, water soluble boron (0.50%) and moisture (0.50%). The starting stoichiometry of powder mixtures was in accordance with the following reaction:
(3)
zTi+BN+(2z −3)B→TiN+(z −1)TiB2
It should be noted that this category of the experiments was performed in argon, implying that the reactant BN provided the only source of nitrogen. Moreover, the SHS process was essentially governed by the solid–solid interactions. Homogenous powder blends with five different molar compositions (including z =1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, and 4.0) were prepared for the synthesis of TiN–TiB2 composites with the TiB2 content varying from 33 to 75mol%. The sample green density was set at 60% TMD for reactant compacts in this reaction system. It is useful to point out that at z =1.5, the reactant BN was the only source of both nitrogen and boron in the reaction.

Typical SHS processes recorded from two reaction systems described above are presented in Fig. 1 (a) and (b), respectively. Fig. 1(a) shows a series of combustion images associated with the reaction of a 60% TMD undiluted sample with a starting stoichiometry of 3Ti+BN at 1.14MPa of nitrogen. It is evident in Fig. 1(a) that upon initiation a distinct combustion front forms and traverses the entire sample in a self-sustaining manner. Moreover, after the passage of the flame front an afterburning glow appears on the sample and lasts for a relatively long period of time (approximately from 1.37 to 3.30s), which is typical of the combustion synthesis involving gaseous reagents. This implies that nitridation in the combustion front is incomplete. As a result, continuous infiltration of nitrogen gas into the porous compact to react with the solid reactant led to the reemergence of burning luminosity after the passage of the flame front.

It was also found in Fig. 1(a) that the propagation of the flame front is accompanied by noticeable shrinkage of the test specimen, due entirely to the melting of the powder compact during the combustion. Melting of Ti-containing reactants is normally encountered in the SHS reactions including combustion synthesis of titanium nitride and carbonitride [19,20], because of the combustion temperature exceeding the melting point of Ti (1660°C). It is useful to note that the degree of sample melting in the course of reaction increases as the Ti content in the green compact increases, such as in the composition of 4Ti+BN. On the contrary, the melting was found to be effectively reduced by the addition of diluent TiN in the sample; that is, the TiN-diluted sample almost retained its original shape and porosity after combustion.

Typical reaction sequence of the Ti–BN–B power compact in argon is photographically presented in Fig. 1(b). When compared to Fig. 1(a), Fig. 1(b) shows a very different burning phenomenon. As indicated in Fig. 1(b), an explosion-like reaction takes place upon ignition and generates a glaring and self-sustaining combustion front traveling along the sample. With the advance of the combustion wave, significant delamination of the burned sample was observed, which resulted in serious structure fracturing and substantial elongation on the end product. In general, the volume increase of combustion synthesized products could be attributed to the intrinsic volume change associated with the conversion of reactants to the final product, and to the vaporization of volatile impurities due to high temperatures [22,23]. It is believed in this study that the vaporization of low boiling point impurities (such as Mg with a boiling point of 1090°C) in amorphous boron produces gases, which expand under high temperatures and are then expelled from the porous sample. The expulsion of gaseous substances with the propagation of the combustion front caused the formation of numerous crevices in the sample and the expansion of sample volume after combustion.

For the reactions in the Ti–BN–N2 system, variations of the flame-front propagation velocity (V f) of undiluted samples with nitrogen pressure, starting stoichiometry of the reactant compacts, and sample green density are presented in Fig. 2 . The increase of flame-front velocity with increasing nitrogen pressure is apparently shown in Fig. 2 for the reactants made up of 3Ti+BN and 4Ti+BN. This is attributed to the increase of nitrogen concentration at the reaction front with increasing pressure. However, the flame-front velocity of the 2Ti+BN sample was found to be nearly independent of the nitrogen pressure. This is most likely caused by the fact that two thirds of nitrogen required for complete conversion from the reactant compact of 2Ti+BN to the composite product of 1.5TiN+0.5TiB2 was supplied by the reactant BN. Consequently, the reaction rate was not significantly enhanced by increasing the nitrogen pressure.

As also indicated in Fig. 2, the decrease of sample density causes an increase in the flame-front velocity. This is because a compact with a lower density not only possesses a larger amount of nitrogen within the porous structure, but provides higher permeability for nitrogen gas to penetrate. The influence of solid-phase dilution with TiN on the flame-front velocity is shown in Fig. 3 , which reveals a decrease in the flame-front velocity with diluent content on account of the decrease of reaction temperature. Numerical values of the flame-front velocities plotted in Figs. 2 and 3 are summarized in Table 1 .

For the combustion of the Ti–BN–B samples in Ar, Fig. 4 shows a substantial increase in the flame-front velocity from 11.7mm/s at the stoichiometry of 2Ti+BN+B (z =2.0) to 19.6mm/s for a 4Ti+BN+5B (z =4.0) compact. The increase of flame velocity with elemental Ti and B contents in the green compact was primarily caused by the highly exothermic reaction in association with the TiB2 formation. That is, the more dominant the reaction between Ti and B in the sample, the higher the flame-front propagation rate. Reaction (3) indicates that as the amounts of Ti and B in the green compact are increased, the mole fraction of BN is correspondingly decreased. Therefore, a lesser amount of energy was consumed to decompose BN during the synthesis reaction. This could also be a reason responsible for the increase of flame-front velocity observed in Fig. 4.

In the reaction system involving Ti–BN compacts under nitrogen, the dependence of the degree of nitridation on the starting stoichiometry, nitrogen pressure, and sample green density is shown in Fig. 5 . Obviously, only the sample of 2Ti+BN reaches complete nitridation at 1.48MPa of nitrogen. For the compacts of 3Ti+BN and 4Ti+BN, however, the amount of nitrogen uptake increases with nitrogen pressure, but is insufficient to reach full nitridation within the pressure range of 0.45–1.48MPa. It is also found in Fig. 5 that the nitridation percentage decreases with increasing Ti/BN ratio of the sample compact. This is partly because a larger amount of nitrogen is required from the surroundings as the reactant contains a higher content of Ti. In part, this is caused by the fact that the extent of sample melting increases when the amount of elemental Ti in the green compact increases. In addition, Fig. 5 shows the augmentation of nitridation percentage resulting from a decrease in the sample density, which is a consequence of the enhancement of access for nitrogen gas during the reaction.

Fig. 6 plots XRD patterns of three typical products obtained from undiluted samples in the Ti–BN–N2 reaction system. As indicated in Fig. 6, the XRD analysis on the final product from the reactant of 2Ti+BN shows no more than two desired phases TiN and TiB2, signifying a case of full conversion. On the other hand, in addition to the phases of TiN and TiB2 intermediate compounds including Ti2N and TiB were detected in the final products obtained from the reactants of 3Ti+BN and 4Ti+BN, suggestive of the incomplete conversion mainly caused by the melting of test samples during the combustion. As mentioned above, the melting of Ti–BN reactant compacts was effectively reduced by the solid-phase dilution with TiN. Therefore, the nitridation of TiN-diluted sample were substantially improved. Fig. 7 shows that complete nitridation is accomplished by a 60% TMD TiN-diluted sample made up of 2.5Ti+BN+0.5TiN (representing 12.5mol% dilution), in comparison to the low degree of nitridation at about 53–68% obtained in undiluted compacts of 3Ti+BN. Fig. 7 also reveals that the sample green density has an effect on the amount of diluent TiN needed for complete nitridation; namely, a lesser amount of diluent was required by a sample with a higher degree of porosity. As indicated in Fig. 7, a 45% TMD compact reaches 100% nitridation under a diluted composition of 2.65Ti+BN+0.35TiN (8.75mol% dilution).

In addition to the sample porosity, the degree of dilution required for full conversion was dependent on the concentration of TiN in the end product. Experimental results show that under a condition of 1.14MPa nitrogen and 60% TMD, the nitridation percentage increases from 42.9% for an undiluted composition of 4Ti+BN to 67.4% for the 3.5Ti+BN+0.5TiN compact, and finally arrives at 100% for the sample of 3Ti+BN+TiN. Fig. 8 shows the XRD patterns corresponding to three TiN–TiB2 composites with TiN mole fractions of 75, 83, and 87.5%, respectively. Among these three compounds, only the composite with 75mol% TiN was directly converted from the 2Ti+BN compact without the addition of diluent TiN. Complete conversion of the other two composite products was achieved under different degrees of dilution by TiN. Fig. 8 indicates that the dilution level increases from 12.5% (at a composition of 2.5Ti+BN+0.5TiN) to 20% (i.e., the sample of 3Ti+BN+TiN) as the TiN concentration of TiN–TiB2 composites increases from 83 to 87.5mol%.

For the reaction system of Ti–BN–B powder compacts in Ar, neither the presence of remaining reactants nor the detection of intermediate phases is indicated by the XRD analysis on burned samples, suggestive of complete conversion from reactants to TiN–TiB2 composites. XRD patterns of the synthesized composites with different TiB2 concentrations of 33–75mol% are presented in Fig. 9 . It is useful to point out formation of the product with a molar ratio of TiN:TiB2 =67:33 from the 1.5Ti+BN sample, within which the reactant BN is the only source of nitrogen and boron. For the other sample compacts shown in Fig. 9, elemental boron powders as the additional reactant other than Ti and BN were employed in order to prepare composites with a TiB2 concentration higher than 33mol%. As shown in Fig. 9, TiN–TiB2 composites with TiB2 mole fractions of 50, 60, and 75% were obtained from the test specimens of 2Ti+BN+B, 2.5Ti+BN+2B, and 4Ti+BN+5B, respectively.

This study represents a successful attempt to prepare in situ TiN–TiB2 composites with a wide range of compositions (including the TiB2 content from 12.5 to 75mol%) through well-designed experiments in two different reaction systems. The increase of TiB2 content improves the hardness and toughness of the TiN–TiB2 composites for the applications of cutting tools and friction resistance materials. Moreover, the electrical conductivity of TiN–TiB2 composites increases with TiN content and this property is beneficial for the composite to be used as a conductive barrier between a silicon device and metal contacts in the semiconductor industry. For the composites with high TiN concentrations of 75–87.5mol%, synthesis reactions under nitrogen pressure were conducted using Ti–BN reactant compacts with or without the addition of TiN as the diluent. On the other hand, the experiments adopting compacted specimens from the Ti–BN–B powder blends were performed in argon for the preparation of composite compounds with a TiB2 molar fraction in the range of 50–75%.

In the former reaction system involving the gaseous reagent N2, the combustion process was characterized by self-sustained reaction with a distinct combustion front propagating in a steady manner. On account of the continuous infiltration of nitrogen gas into the sample compact, an afterburning reaction took place after the passage of the flame front. In addition, in the case of lacking TiN dilution the propagation of the flame front was accompanied by significant melting of the sample compact, resulting in a decrease in the sample permeability and a poor degree of nitridation. Test specimens diluted by a proper amount of TiN was shown to be highly beneficial in the reduction of sample melting and hence the enhancement of nitrogen uptake. The extent of dilution was affected by the sample green density and the mole fraction of TiN in final composites. Experimental results indicated that the TiN–TiB2 composite with 75mol% TiN was directly synthesized from the reaction of an undiluted compact made up of 2Ti+BN in 1.48MPa nitrogen. For the preparation of composites with TiN contents as high as 83 and 87.5mol%, it was demonstrated that complete conversion in 1.14MPa nitrogen was achieved by 60% TMD TiN-diluted sample with initial compositions of 2.5Ti+BN+0.5TiN and 3Ti+BN+TiN, respectively. Due to the fact that nitrogen uptake was augmented when the sample density was reduced, a 45% TMD sample at a dilution level of 2.65Ti+BN+0.35TiN (8.75mol% TiN) was shown to attain complete nitridation as the 2.5Ti+BN+0.5TiN (12.5mol%) compact at 60% TMD.

In the latter reaction system with Ti–BN–B powder compacts in Ar, highly exothermic reactions led to a glaring combustion front similar to that observed in the former SHS system. However, vaporization and expulsion of low boiling point impurities in amorphous boron during the combustion generated numerous crevices in the end products. In contrast to the noticeable shrinkage of Ti–BN samples in nitrogen, the Ti–BN–B powder compacts burning in Ar exhibited substantial volume expansion after combustion. XRD analysis verified complete conversion of TiN–TiB2 composites with TiB2 content between 50 and 75mol% from the combustion of Ti–BN–B compacts in Ar. Despite the fact that the boron powders used in this study contained some impurities, the XRD analysis indicated the absence of these impurities in the synthesized products. Therefore, this study could confirm that the impurities in the boron powders have no influence on the production of TiN–TiB2 composites, except for the formation of some cracks in the final products. In addition, direct formation of a TiN–33.3mol% TiB2 composite in Ar was confirmed by the reaction of a 1.5Ti+BN sample, within which the reactant BN served as the only source of nitrogen and boron.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was sponsored by the National Science Council of Taiwan, ROC, under the grant of NSC 94-2212-E-212-018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ Reactions for Synthesis of Composites, Ceramics, and Intermetallics
